title: Rice and peas
subtitle: Jamaican coconut-and-kidney-bean rice, the Sunday side
excerpt: Long-grain rice cooked in coconut milk with kidney beans, scotch bonnet, spring onion, and thyme. The essential Jamaican accompaniment, served beside jerk chicken, brown stew, or curry goat every Sunday.
sourceNotes: Rice and peas is eaten every Sunday across Jamaica and throughout the Jamaican diaspora. The 'peas' are kidney beans, confusingly for British readers, legumes are called peas in Jamaican English. The dish is one of the staple carbohydrate sides that appears alongside jerk chicken, oxtail, and curries. The coconut milk is non-negotiable; it gives the rice its characteristic creaminess and mild sweetness. The scotch bonnet is added whole and removed before serving, flavouring the rice without making it searingly hot.

content.text: Rice and peas is Sunday food in Jamaica. The kidney beans ('peas' in Jamaican English) are simmered with coconut milk, spring onion, thyme, and a whole scotch bonnet that perfumes the rice without splitting its heat into every mouthful. The coconut milk makes the grains fluffy and slightly glossy, a different texture entirely from plain boiled rice.

The method is simple: cook the beans first if using dried, then simmer everything together until the liquid is absorbed and the rice is cooked through. The key is the ratio, too much liquid and you get wet, porridge-like rice; too little and the bottom catches before the top is done. The scotch bonnet stays whole throughout; pierce it only if you want a hotter result.

items.name: Long-grain rice
items.prepNote: rinsed until the water runs clear

items.name: Spring onion
items.prepNote: tied in a bundle with kitchen string

items.name: Scotch bonnet
items.prepNote: whole, uncut, or pierced once for more heat

content.text: Put the {{coconut-milk}} of coconut milk and 300 ml of water in a large saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{kidney-beans}}, the bundle of {{spring-onion}} spring onions, the whole {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet, {{thyme-fresh}} of thyme, and {{garlic}} of crushed garlic. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook for 5 minutes to let the flavours meld.

Add the {{long-grain-rice}} of rinsed rice, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{black-pepper-ground}} of black pepper. Stir once to combine. Bring back to a simmer, then reduce the heat to its lowest setting, cover tightly, and cook for 18 minutes without lifting the lid. Take off the heat and leave to steam, still covered, for 5 minutes.
Remove and discard the spring onion bundle, scotch bonnet, and thyme sprigs. Fluff the rice gently with a fork. Taste for salt and serve.

items.fix: Use the lowest possible hob setting. Place a piece of folded kitchen paper between the pot and lid to absorb excess steam and improve the seal
items.cause: Heat too high; lid not sealing tightly
items.symptom: Rice catching on the bottom of the pan

items.fix: Remove the lid and increase the heat to medium for 2 to 3 minutes to evaporate the excess liquid, stirring occasionally
items.cause: Too much liquid or lid not on tight

items.fix: Add a whole uncut scotch bonnet next time and check it remains intact. The flavour permeates without the heat if it stays whole. Once too hot, balance with extra coconut milk and a squeeze of lime
items.cause: Scotch bonnet pierced or burst during cooking

items.fix: Season with salt after fluffing. Use full-fat coconut milk and make sure the ratio is 400 ml coconut milk plus 300 ml water for 300 g rice
items.cause: Insufficient salt or coconut milk proportion too low

content.text: In Jamaica, Sunday is rice-and-peas day. The dish appears alongside whatever is in the pot: oxtail, curry goat, jerk chicken, brown stew. It is so closely associated with Sunday cooking that it functions as a signal for the meal. Outside Jamaica, it has become the Jamaican carbohydrate in every Jamaican restaurant and home kitchen in the diaspora. The version with red kidney beans is Jamaican; Trinidadian rice uses pigeon peas, which give a more earthy, less creamy result. Gungo peas (pigeon peas) also appear in Jamaican cooking around Christmas.
